claims.text: Physicists consider time travel to the future scientifically proven while travel to the past remains open.

evidence.quote: time travel to the future is not only possible

claims.rationale: Correct summary of special/general relativity — time dilation (high-velocity and gravitational) is experimentally confirmed (atomic clocks on GPS satellites, muon lifetimes, etc.). Travel to past remains theoretically contentious (requires exotic matter / closed timelike curves).

claims.text: A man named Rudolph Fentz appeared in Times Square in 1950 carrying 1870s artifacts and died after being struck by a taxi.

evidence.quote:
Times Square at 11, 15 p.m.
that evening, and witness described him as gawking and looking around at

claims.rationale: Host explicitly debunks this in the verdict: Rudolph Fentz is a character from Jack Finney's 1951 Collier's short story 'I'm Scared,' not a real person. Near-zero directTruth.

claims.text: The Rudolph Fentz story originated as an excerpt from Jack Finney's short story 'I'm Scared' published in Collier's magazine in 1951.

evidence.quote:
excerpt from a short story called I'm Scared, which was written by Jack
Finney, you might know, is the author of The Body Snatcher

claims.rationale: Well-documented literary history: Finney's 'I'm Scared' was published in Collier's September 15, 1951. Chris Aubeck's research established this as the origin of the Fentz urban legend.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Directly identifies Fentz as fictional, with a datable publication origin.

claims.text: In 2003 the FBI arrested a 44-year-old Andrew Carlson for insider trading after he turned $800 into $350 million with 126 consecutive winning trades.

evidence.quote:
2003, the FBI arrested forty four year old Andrew Carlson for suspicion of insider
trading and just two weeks, Carlson had made one hundr

claims.rationale: Host's verdict section reveals this story originated as a Weekly World News satirical piece. Zero-sum: no FBI arrest of an Andrew Carlson matching these particulars has ever occurred on record.

claims.text: Andrew Carlson confessed that he time-traveled back 250 years from the future with foreknowledge of winning stock trades.

evidence.quote:
Carlson stated he traveled back in time from two hundred and fifty years in the future,
bringing knowledge of hig

claims.rationale: A fictional character cannot have made a real confession. Host debunks Carlson as a Weekly World News invention in the verdict section.

claims.text: The Andrew Carlson time-traveler story originated as a fictional piece in the satirical Weekly World News and was later reported as fact by mainstream outlets.

evidence.quote:
originated as a fictional piece in Weekly World News, a satirical newspaper.
Later, the story was picked up by Y

claims.rationale: Well-documented urban-legend trajectory: Weekly World News (a supermarket tabloid explicitly publishing satire) ran the piece, and Yahoo News/others amplified it uncritically. Host's verdict aligns with mainstream fact-checking.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Identifying the origin as satirical tabloid fiction directly invalidates the time-travel confession narrative.

claims.text: A 1940 photograph from the South Fork Bridge reopening in British Columbia shows a 'hipster' man with anachronistically modern haircut, sunglasses, graphic tee and camera.

evidence.quote:
1940 at the
reopening of the South Fork Bridge in Canada.
When the museum digitized the photo and put it online

claims.rationale: The photograph itself is real and held at the Braylon Pioneer Museum. The 'anachronistic' features are genuinely visible in the image — the dispute is whether they're actually anachronistic, not whether the photo exists.

claims.text: The 'Time Traveling Hipster' photo is explained by the fact that t-shirts with logos, small cameras, and wraparound sunglasses were all available in 1940 — the jersey is a Montreal Maroons hockey team shirt.

evidence.quote:
Montreal Maroons embroidered jersey.
And the Marines were a hockey team that played in the NHL from nineteen twenty

claims.rationale: Host's debunking aligns with mainstream fact-checking: the Montreal Maroons existed in the NHL 1924-1938, small portable cameras (e.g. Kodak Bantam, Argus A) existed by 1940, and protective-side-shield sunglasses were available. All individual items are era-plausible.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Provides a mundane explanation: the photo is real but none of the items are actually out-of-period.

claims.text: A woman in the 1928 Charlie Chaplin Circus premiere footage is seen talking into what looks like a cell phone.

evidence.quote:
ime agents.
She's saying this really sucks.
Let's get in and out burger Inkscape.
Maybe in a few years later.
This happened a

claims.rationale: Footage does exist in the DVD extras and the woman is visible holding something to her ear. Whether that's a cell phone, hearing aid, or nothing at all is the contested interpretation. Claim is about what the footage appears to show.

claims.text: The woman in the Chaplin clip is most likely using an early ear trumpet or hearing aid rather than a phone.

evidence.quote:
thought to
be using an early hearing aid, though I'm not sure why she's talking to herself and
looking

claims.rationale: The 1920s saw portable carbon-microphone hearing-aid devices (e.g. Western Electric's Vactuphone / Siemens auricular trumpets); fit the shape seen. Host presents this as the leading skeptic explanation.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Offers the mundane hearing-aid explanation instead of a phone.

claims.text: A 1938 Dupont-factory woman's apparent cell-phone call was actually a Dupont-developed experimental wireless phone used by worker Gertrude Jones.

evidence.quote:
Gertrude Jones
and she was working for Dupont and using an experimental wireless telephone developed by
the compan

claims.rationale: Host himself pushes back on this explanation — 1938 battery tech couldn't power a wireless device that small, and the walkie-talkie was not patented until 1936 and required a 30-pound backpack. The family's claim is itself unverified and convenient.

claims.text: The carved astronaut on the Cathedral of Salamanca is not evidence of medieval time travel — it was added by the artist during a 1992 renovation.

evidence.quote:
cathedral was renovated and restored in 1992, the artist added their own
modern touches

claims.rationale: Well-documented: the astronaut (and an ice-cream-eating gargoyle and dragon) were added by stonemason Jeronimo Garcia during the 1992 restoration of the New Cathedral of Salamanca. Host's verdict is factually airtight.

claims.text: An ancient Swiss watch was found inside a 400-year-old sealed Ming Dynasty tomb in Shanxi, northern China.

evidence.quote:
ancient watch found in the Chinese tomb.
That's a straight up hoax.
Photoshopped debunked the astronaut

claims.rationale: Host debunks within the same sentence — 'that's a straight up hoax.' Photoshopped image, well-established in fact-checker circles. Near-zero directTruth.

claims.text: Humans are so obsessed with time travel because time itself is the most valuable thing we have and cannot be reclaimed once lost.

evidence.quote:
Time is the most valuable thing you have.
Spend it wisely. Thanks for hanging out with us today.
My name is A.J. That Tigerfish, this has been th

claims.rationale: Host's closing opinion framing. Philosophical claim; not a testable proposition. Omitting directTruth because this is a value judgment.
